meta.title: dig — agents that keep your knowledge base in order
meta.description: Open-source CLI agent harness for knowledge bases: find, organize, dedupe, label, version, reconcile. You set the policy; dig keeps the library converged. Local-first, AI-optional, MIT.

docs.meta_description: dig user docs: quickstart, the full command reference, the policy file, and how to drive dig from any agent (MCP, the Vercel AI SDK, and the TypeScript/Python SDKs). Local-first, reversible.
docs.title: Docs
docs.subtitle: Everything you need to drive dig — the first run, every command, the policy file, and how any agent plugs in.
docs.quickstart_body: Index a directory, search it, reorganize by policy, and step back — all reversible.
docs.agent_title: Let your agent set it up
docs.agent_body: dig is built to be agent-operated. Once your agent has the dig skill, you don't run the commands — you ask. The skill is dig's canonical instruction set; it teaches the agent the whole surface and the safety rules, so it can do the setup for you, reversibly.
docs.agent_install_body: The Claude Code and Codex plugins bundle the dig skill — one command installs the skill and the dig mcp server. Any other agent can read the portable skill at skills/dig/SKILL.md, or register the dig mcp server for the same tools.
docs.agent_ask_title: 2 · Ask it to set up dig
docs.agent_ask_body: With the skill loaded, your agent knows the whole surface. Point it at a directory and it will:
docs.commands_body: Read commands (find, export, drift, log) support --json for other harnesses. Mutating commands (org, dedup, reconcile, merge, undo) journal every change.
docs.policy_body: The policy lives at .dig/policy.toml and travels with the KB. Validation is strict — unknown keys and path escapes fail loudly.
docs.integrate_body: Drive dig from any agent, app, or script — over MCP, the typed SDKs on a local daemon, or the CLI directly. One surface, three doors.
docs.integrate_mcp_body: Register the dig mcp server and your agent gets find, recall, retain, drift, log, export, org, reconcile, and undo as tools.
docs.integrate_aisdk_body: Expose dig as AI SDK tools so the model searches the knowledge base and remembers across sessions, locally.
docs.integrate_sdk_body: Dependency-light clients over the local dig serve daemon — the same surface, including recall and retain memory.

install.meta_description: Install dig in one line — curl the installer, go install, or download a prebuilt binary. Local-first, checksum-verified, no account required.
install.title: Install dig
install.subtitle: One line, no account, no cloud. The installer detects your OS and architecture, verifies the checksum, and drops dig on your PATH.
install.agent_note: Already using a coding agent? Pick it and copy the 10-second install — plugin, MCP config, or SDK.
install.primary_note: Downloads the latest release for your platform from GitHub and verifies its SHA256 checksum. Set DIG_VERSION to pin a version, DIG_INSTALL_DIR to choose where it lands.
install.go_note: Builds from source — handy if you already have a Go toolchain.
install.binary_note: Grab a checksummed archive for linux, macOS, or Windows (amd64 / arm64) from the releases page and put dig on your PATH.
install.next_note: Point dig at a directory, scan it, and search — every change is journaled and reversible.

leaderboard.meta_description: dig's measured retrieval scores on LongMemEval, LoCoMo, and BEAM — hybrid hit@5 98.0% on LongMemEval, beating the published bar, fully local on CPU.
leaderboard.title: dig beats the published bar — fully local
leaderboard.subtitle: Retrieval recall through dig's real scan → store → index pipeline, on CPU with small open embedding models. No reranker, no LLM in the loop.
leaderboard.headline_bar: vs. {system}'s published {bar}% on the same benchmark and model class
leaderboard.baselines_intro: Shown for context. Retrieval recall and LLM-judged QA accuracy are different measurements — recall is structurally higher, since finding the evidence is easier than answering from it. dig reports retrieval metrics because dig is the retrieval layer serving knowledge-base management; answering belongs to the agent driving it.
leaderboard.method_body: Each benchmark is mapped onto a real dig knowledge base (one file per session or turn), indexed with the production FTS + vector pipeline, and scored per question against its evidence with standard IR metrics (recall@k, hit@k, NDCG@10, MRR). FTS is the deterministic default; vector and hybrid (Reciprocal Rank Fusion) opt in via a [retrieval] policy. Full methodology and reproduction commands live in docs/evals.md.

hero.title: The knowledge base your coding agent keeps in order
hero.subtitle: dig keeps a knowledge base organized to your rules — deduped, versioned, every change reversible — and retrieves it fast over MCP for Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, any agent. A KB kept this clean is one your agent recalls across sessions. Runs fully on your machine.
hero.proof: 98% hit@5 retrieval on LongMemEval — fully local

problem.title: Every knowledge base rots
problem.body: Files land in the wrong place. Names drift from convention. Duplicates pile up. Keeping a library tidy is real, recurring work — and the tools that could automate it are either single-purpose or too dangerous to let loose on real files.
problem.agitate: Movers move, organizers rename, linters flag. None of them manage the structure of a living library and keep it converged on your rules.

how.title: Declare the policy. dig does the librarian's job.
how.subtitle: Find, organize, dedupe, label, version, reconcile — driven by one policy file, never ad hoc.
how.step1_body: Rules, invariants, and workflows in one TOML file: where files belong, what they're named, which labels they get.
how.step2_body: dig compares the library against policy, reports drift, and brings it back — automatically where safe, by proposal where not.
how.step3_body: Every change is journaled as a reversible changeset. Preview with dry-run, browse with log, step back with undo.

features.title: One content-addressed core. Six jobs.
features.subtitle: Dedupe, versioning, isolation, and merge fall out of one store design — not six bolted-on features.
features.f1_body: Indexed, ranked find across the whole library — built for humans and for other harnesses via --json output.
features.f2_body: Declarative rules make the tree match your conventions — readable, like a librarian shelving books.
features.f3_body: Identical content hashes identically. Duplicates are detected by construction and collapsed per policy.
features.f4_body: Full history of every move, rename, and label. Any change is reversible with dig undo.
features.f5_body: Policy is desired state. dig reports what is misfiled, misnamed, duplicated, or unlabeled — then reconciles.
features.f6_body: Many agents, one library: isolated work views, automatic merge of disjoint changes, human escalation only on real conflicts.

principles.title: Built like infrastructure, not an app
principles.p1_body: Runs fully on your machine. No telemetry, no cloud requirement.
principles.p2_body: The deterministic core works with no model at all. Opt in with any OpenAI-compatible endpoint — a small local model is enough.
principles.p3_body: The command line is the whole interface. Any agent harness drives dig by running commands.
principles.p4_body: Pure Go, cgo-free, cross-compiles everywhere. No runtime to install.
principles.p5_body: Keep editing with Obsidian, Finder, or your editor. dig reconciles around you — it never locks you out.
principles.p6_body: Eight typed seams for storage, events, extraction, commands, and more. Small core, rich edges.

faq.title: Questions you would ask
faq.q1: Is dig a RAG or Q&A assistant?
faq.a1: No. dig governs where files live and what they are called — it manages structure. Retrieval serves management, not chat. That lane is deliberate.
faq.a2: No. With AI mode off, the core is fully deterministic and offline. AI is opt-in and only makes small, bounded judgments while dig's tools do the structural work.
faq.a3: Never silently. dig observes direct edits, folds them into history, and reconciles them against policy. A deliberate human change is escalated, not overridden.
faq.a4: Yes. The CLI ships the full lifecycle — scan, find, organize, dedupe, drift, reconcile, watch, export, undo — plus opt-in semantic search that beats the published retrieval benchmarks (98.0% hit@5 on LongMemEval, fully local). It's pre-1.0 and moving fast; install it and follow what lands next.
faq.q6: Does dig work with Claude Code, Codex, or other agents?
faq.a6: Yes. dig ships a Claude Code plugin (/plugin marketplace add vllnt/dig), a dig mcp server for any MCP client (Cursor, Codex, opencode, …), entry docs for Codex and Gemini, and TypeScript/Python SDKs. The integrations page has the exact 10-second install per agent.

cta.title: Follow the build
cta.body: dig is being built in the open, phase by phase — the safety spine first, destructive features only on top of it. Star the repo to watch it land.

home_integrations.title: Works with your agent
home_integrations.subtitle: dig is harness-agnostic — one MCP server and a portable skill plug it into every coding agent. Pick yours for the exact install.

integrations.meta_title: Integrations — drive dig from any agent
integrations.meta_description: Plug dig into Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, the Vercel AI SDK, or any MCP client — local, reversible memory and retrieval for your coding agent, installed in seconds.
integrations.title: Drive dig from your agent
integrations.subtitle: One MCP server, a portable skill, and thin per-harness shims. dig stays the data + memory layer — never the agent or the model.

footer.tagline: An open-source agent harness for knowledge bases.

consent.message: We use privacy-respecting analytics to improve dig.
